story:
The Unicorn stood back in the alleys of Hranda, away from easy eyes. It took in the drinkers other inns turned away—thieves, cutthroats, beggars, strays—and yet there was never much trouble. The secret was simple. The men who drank there knew they had no welcome at The Black Cow, The Welcome Arms, or any other decent house. So they kept their own order. At the first hint of a fight, the trouble was carried out by the very hands that had started it. That suited the landlord, Piotr Garim. He was a large man, tall and barrel-chested, his fair hair going grey, but he hated violence.

People came to Hranda to rise or to run. Garim had run—from a past he could not quite kill. He had bought the failing inn and kept to himself. Most nights passed like any other until, late one evening, a man in a heavy cloak came in and sat by the fire. He stayed there after the last of the regulars had gone. Garim gathered the empty mugs and waited, heart thudding, for the man to rise.

“It’s been quite a while,” the stranger said. He laughed at Garim’s name. “I thought you would at least have changed it.”

“You must have the wrong Piotr Garim,” Garim replied. Avoiding eye contact.

“I don't think so,” the man in the cloak said.

There was nothing he could say other than try to deny it all, but that would be useless. He recognised the man as Alex Turgov just as well as the man identified him. “What do you want.”

“To be sure the secret stays buried.”

“It already is,” Garim said.

“I don’t believe you,” Turgov answered, drawing a knife and standing. “Did you think you could hide here forever?”

Garim backed away, certain the past had found his throat. Then came the thunder of boots behind the bar. Two big regulars burst through. Mugs smashed on the flags; shards of pottery skittered. A chair cracked, a table went over, and in a tangle of arms and cloak and steel, Alex Turgov fell with his own knife driven deep into his chest.

Silence followed, save the settling of splinters. Garim sagged into a chair while the two men righted the table and swept aside the broken pieces.

“Sorry about the mess,” one muttered, more practical than shocked. “When your friend in the cloak didn’t come straight out, we thought we’d go round the back and check on you.”

“Looks like we were right to be concerned,” the other said.

Garim managed a thank you that felt too small. The second man knelt, pulled back the dead man’s hood, and revealed the ugly rope-burn scar around the neck. There are not many men who cheat the hangman. This, then, was one. And the hangman—the only one left who could name him—had been pouring his ale.

“Never seen him before,” Garim said at last. He spoke it for the room, and for himself. The two men nodded and went about setting The Unicorn to rights. The body would be taken care of; in Hranda, such things were handled with a quiet sort of mercy.

When the door was bolted and the ashes stirred down, Piotr Garim stood alone in the lamplight. He had left his old life behind, but it had followed him into the back streets. Now, with the one man who could drag it back into daylight gone, he felt the grip of it slacken. He could begin, at last, to forget.
